6789										 cutoffs->OldestXmin)));
6790			*flags |= FRM_INVALIDATE_XMAX;
6791			pagefrz->freeze_required = true;
6792			return InvalidTransactionId;
6793		}
6794
6795		/* Have to keep updater XID as new xmax */
6796		*flags |= FRM_RETURN_IS_XID;
6797		pagefrz->freeze_required = true;
6798		return update_xact;
6799	}
6800
6801	/*
6802	 * Some member(s) of this Multi may be below FreezeLimit xid cutoff, so we
6803	 * need to walk the whole members array to figure out what to do, if
6804	 * anything.
6805	 */
6806	nmembers =
6807		GetMultiXactIdMembers(multi, &members, false,
6808							  HEAP_XMAX_IS_LOCKED_ONLY(t_infomask));
6809	if (nmembers <= 0)
6810	{
6811		/* Nothing worth keeping */
6812		*flags |= FRM_INVALIDATE_XMAX;
6813		pagefrz->freeze_required = true;
6814		return InvalidTransactionId;
6815	}
6816
6817	/*
6818	 * The FRM_NOOP case is the only case where we might need to ratchet back
6819	 * FreezePageRelfrozenXid or FreezePageRelminMxid.  It is also the only
6820	 * case where our caller might ratchet back its NoFreezePageRelfrozenXid
6821	 * or NoFreezePageRelminMxid "no freeze" trackers to deal with a multi.
6822	 * FRM_NOOP handling should result in the NewRelfrozenXid/NewRelminMxid
6823	 * trackers managed by VACUUM being ratcheting back by xmax to the degree
6824	 * required to make it safe to leave xmax undisturbed, independent of
6825	 * whether or not page freezing is triggered somewhere else.
6826	 *
6827	 * Our policy is to force freezing in every case other than FRM_NOOP,
6828	 * which obviates the need to maintain either set of trackers, anywhere.
6829	 * Every other case will reliably execute a freeze plan for xmax that
6830	 * either replaces xmax with an XID/MXID >= OldestXmin/OldestMxact, or
6831	 * sets xmax to an InvalidTransactionId XID, rendering xmax fully frozen.
6832	 * (VACUUM's NewRelfrozenXid/NewRelminMxid trackers are initialized with
6833	 * OldestXmin/OldestMxact, so later values never need to be tracked here.)
6834	 */
6835	need_replace = false;
6836	FreezePageRelfrozenXid = pagefrz->FreezePageRelfrozenXid;
6837	for (int i = 0; i < nmembers; i++)
6838	{
6839		TransactionId xid = members[i].xid;
6840
6841		Assert(!TransactionIdPrecedes(xid, cutoffs->relfrozenxid));
6842
6843		if (TransactionIdPrecedes(xid, cutoffs->FreezeLimit))
6844		{
6845			/* Can't violate the FreezeLimit postcondition */
6846			need_replace = true;
6847			break;
6848		}
6849		if (TransactionIdPrecedes(xid, FreezePageRelfrozenXid))
6850			FreezePageRelfrozenXid = xid;
6851	}
6852
6853	/* Can't violate the MultiXactCutoff postcondition, either */
6854	if (!need_replace)
6855		need_replace = MultiXactIdPrecedes(multi, cutoffs->MultiXactCutoff);
6856
6857	if (!need_replace)
6858	{
6859		/*
6860		 * vacuumlazy.c might ratchet back NewRelminMxid, NewRelfrozenXid, or
6861		 * both together to make it safe to retain this particular multi after
6862		 * freezing its page
6863		 */
6864		*flags |= FRM_NOOP;
6865		pagefrz->FreezePageRelfrozenXid = FreezePageRelfrozenXid;
6866		if (MultiXactIdPrecedes(multi, pagefrz->FreezePageRelminMxid))
6867			pagefrz->FreezePageRelminMxid = multi;
6868		pfree(members);
6869		return multi;
6870	}
6871
6872	/*
6873	 * Do a more thorough second pass over the multi to figure out which
6874	 * member XIDs actually need to be kept.  Checking the precise status of
6875	 * individual members might even show that we don't need to keep anything.
6876	 * That is quite possible even though the Multi must be >= OldestMxact,
6877	 * since our second pass only keeps member XIDs when it's truly necessary;
6878	 * even member XIDs >= OldestXmin often won't be kept by second pass.
6879	 */
6880	nnewmembers = 0;
6881	newmembers = palloc(sizeof(MultiXactMember) * nmembers);
6882	has_lockers = false;
6883	update_xid = InvalidTransactionId;
6884	update_committed = false;
6885
6886	/*
6887	 * Determine whether to keep each member xid, or to ignore it instead
6888	 */
6889	for (int i = 0; i < nmembers; i++)
6890	{
6891		TransactionId xid = members[i].xid;
6892		MultiXactStatus mstatus = members[i].status;
6893
6894		Assert(!TransactionIdPrecedes(xid, cutoffs->relfrozenxid));
6895
6896		if (!ISUPDATE_from_mxstatus(mstatus))
6897		{
6898			/*
6899			 * Locker XID (not updater XID).  We only keep lockers that are
6900			 * still running.
6901			 */
6902			if (TransactionIdIsCurrentTransactionId(xid) ||
6903				TransactionIdIsInProgress(xid))
6904			{
6905				if (TransactionIdPrecedes(xid, cutoffs->OldestXmin))
6906					ereport(ERROR,
6907							(errcode(ERRCODE_DATA_CORRUPTED),
6908							 errmsg_internal("multixact %u contains running locker XID %u from before removable cutoff %u",
6909											 multi, xid,
6910											 cutoffs->OldestXmin)));
6911				newmembers[nnewmembers++] = members[i];
6912				has_lockers = true;
6913			}
6914
6915			continue;
6916		}
6917
6918		/*
6919		 * Updater XID (not locker XID).  Should we keep it?
6920		 *
6921		 * Since the tuple wasn't totally removed when vacuum pruned, the
6922		 * update Xid cannot possibly be older than OldestXmin cutoff unless
6923		 * the updater XID aborted.  If the updater transaction is known
6924		 * aborted or crashed then it's okay to ignore it, otherwise not.
6925		 *
6926		 * In any case the Multi should never contain two updaters, whatever
6927		 * their individual commit status.  Check for that first, in passing.
6928		 */
6929		if (TransactionIdIsValid(update_xid))
6930			ereport(ERROR,
6931					(errcode(ERRCODE_DATA_CORRUPTED),
6932					 errmsg_internal("multixact %u has two or more updating members",
6933									 multi),
6934					 errdetail_internal("First updater XID=%u second updater XID=%u.",
6935										update_xid, xid)));
6936
6937		/*
6938		 * As with all tuple visibility routines, it's critical to test
6939		 * TransactionIdIsInProgress before TransactionIdDidCommit, because of
6940		 * race conditions explained in detail in heapam_visibility.c.
6941		 */
6942		if (TransactionIdIsCurrentTransactionId(xid) ||
6943			TransactionIdIsInProgress(xid))
6944			update_xid = xid;
6945		else if (TransactionIdDidCommit(xid))
6946		{
6947			/*
6948			 * The transaction committed, so we can tell caller to set
6949			 * HEAP_XMAX_COMMITTED.  (We can only do this because we know the
6950			 * transaction is not running.)
6951			 */
6952			update_committed = true;
6953			update_xid = xid;
6954		}
6955		else
6956		{
6957			/*
6958			 * Not in progress, not committed -- must be aborted or crashed;
6959			 * we can ignore it.
6960			 */
6961			continue;
6962		}
6963
6964		/*
6965		 * We determined that updater must be kept -- add it to pending new
6966		 * members list
6967		 */
6968		if (TransactionIdPrecedes(xid, cutoffs->OldestXmin))
6969			ereport(ERROR,
6970					(errcode(ERRCODE_DATA_CORRUPTED),
6971					 errmsg_internal("multixact %u contains committed update XID %u from before removable cutoff %u",
6972									 multi, xid, cutoffs->OldestXmin)));
6973		newmembers[nnewmembers++] = members[i];
6974	}
6975
6976	pfree(members);
6977
6978	/*
6979	 * Determine what to do with caller's multi based on information gathered
6980	 * during our second pass
6981	 */
6982	if (nnewmembers == 0)
6983	{
6984		/* Nothing worth keeping */
6985		*flags |= FRM_INVALIDATE_XMAX;
6986		newxmax = InvalidTransactionId;
6987	}
6988	else if (TransactionIdIsValid(update_xid) && !has_lockers)
6989	{
6990		/*
6991		 * If there's a single member and it's an update, pass it back alone
6992		 * without creating a new Multi.  (XXX we could do this when there's a
6993		 * single remaining locker, too, but that would complicate the API too
6994		 * much; moreover, the case with the single updater is more
6995		 * interesting, because those are longer-lived.)
6996		 */
6997		Assert(nnewmembers == 1);
6998		*flags |= FRM_RETURN_IS_XID;
6999		if (update_committed)
7000			*flags |= FRM_MARK_COMMITTED;
7001		newxmax = update_xid;
7002	}
7003	else
7004	{
7005		/*
7006		 * Create a new multixact with the surviving members of the previous
7007		 * one, to set as new Xmax in the tuple
7008		 */
7009		newxmax = MultiXactIdCreateFromMembers(nnewmembers, newmembers);
7010		*flags |= FRM_RETURN_IS_MULTI;
7011	}
7012
7013	pfree(newmembers);
7014
7015	pagefrz->freeze_required = true;
7016	return newxmax;
7017}
7018
7019/*
7020 * heap_prepare_freeze_tuple
7021 *
7022 * Check to see whether any of the XID fields of a tuple (xmin, xmax, xvac)
7023 * are older than the OldestXmin and/or OldestMxact freeze cutoffs.  If so,
7024 * setup enough state (in the *frz output argument) to enable caller to
7025 * process this tuple as part of freezing its page, and return true.  Return
7026 * false if nothing can be changed about the tuple right now.
7027 *
7028 * Also sets *totally_frozen to true if the tuple will be totally frozen once
7029 * caller executes returned freeze plan (or if the tuple was already totally
7030 * frozen by an earlier VACUUM).  This indicates that there are no remaining
7031 * XIDs or MultiXactIds that will need to be processed by a future VACUUM.
7032 *
7033 * VACUUM caller must assemble HeapTupleFreeze freeze plan entries for every
7034 * tuple that we returned true for, and then execute freezing.  Caller must
7035 * initialize pagefrz fields for page as a whole before first call here for
7036 * each heap page.
7037 *
7038 * VACUUM caller decides on whether or not to freeze the page as a whole.
7039 * We'll often prepare freeze plans for a page that caller just discards.
7040 * However, VACUUM doesn't always get to make a choice; it must freeze when
7041 * pagefrz.freeze_required is set, to ensure that any XIDs < FreezeLimit (and
7042 * MXIDs < MultiXactCutoff) can never be left behind.  We help to make sure
7043 * that VACUUM always follows that rule.
7044 *
7045 * We sometimes force freezing of xmax MultiXactId values long before it is
7046 * strictly necessary to do so just to ensure the FreezeLimit postcondition.
7047 * It's worth processing MultiXactIds proactively when it is cheap to do so,
7048 * and it's convenient to make that happen by piggy-backing it on the "force
7049 * freezing" mechanism.  Conversely, we sometimes delay freezing MultiXactIds
7050 * because it is expensive right now (though only when it's still possible to
7051 * do so without violating the FreezeLimit/MultiXactCutoff postcondition).
7052 *
7053 * It is assumed that the caller has checked the tuple with
7054 * HeapTupleSatisfiesVacuum() and determined that it is not HEAPTUPLE_DEAD
7055 * (else we should be removing the tuple, not freezing it).
7056 *
7057 * NB: This function has side effects: it might allocate a new MultiXactId.
7058 * It will be set as tuple's new xmax when our *frz output is processed within
7059 * heap_execute_freeze_tuple later on.  If the tuple is in a shared buffer
7060 * then caller had better have an exclusive lock on it already.
7061 */
7062bool
7063heap_prepare_freeze_tuple(HeapTupleHeader tuple,
7064						  const struct VacuumCutoffs *cutoffs,
7065						  HeapPageFreeze *pagefrz,
7066						  HeapTupleFreeze *frz, bool *totally_frozen)
7067{
7068	bool		xmin_already_frozen = false,
7069				xmax_already_frozen = false;
7070	bool		freeze_xmin = false,
7071				replace_xvac = false,
7072				replace_xmax = false,
7073				freeze_xmax = false;
7074	TransactionId xid;
7075
7076	frz->xmax = HeapTupleHeaderGetRawXmax(tuple);
7077	frz->t_infomask2 = tuple->t_infomask2;
7078	frz->t_infomask = tuple->t_infomask;
7079	frz->frzflags = 0;
7080	frz->checkflags = 0;
7081
7082	/*
7083	 * Process xmin, while keeping track of whether it's already frozen, or
7084	 * will become frozen iff our freeze plan is executed by caller (could be
7085	 * neither).
7086	 */
7087	xid = HeapTupleHeaderGetXmin(tuple);
7088	if (!TransactionIdIsNormal(xid))
7089		xmin_already_frozen = true;
7090	else
7091	{
7092		if (TransactionIdPrecedes(xid, cutoffs->relfrozenxid))
7093			ereport(ERROR,
7094					(errcode(ERRCODE_DATA_CORRUPTED),
7095					 errmsg_internal("found xmin %u from before relfrozenxid %u",
7096									 xid, cutoffs->relfrozenxid)));
7097
7098		/* Will set freeze_xmin flags in freeze plan below */
7099		freeze_xmin = TransactionIdPrecedes(xid, cutoffs->OldestXmin);
7100
7101		/* Verify that xmin committed if and when freeze plan is executed */
7102		if (freeze_xmin)
7103			frz->checkflags |= HEAP_FREEZE_CHECK_XMIN_COMMITTED;
7104	}
7105
7106	/*
7107	 * Old-style VACUUM FULL is gone, but we have to process xvac for as long
7108	 * as we support having MOVED_OFF/MOVED_IN tuples in the database
7109	 */
7110	xid = HeapTupleHeaderGetXvac(tuple);
7111	if (TransactionIdIsNormal(xid))
7112	{
7113		Assert(TransactionIdPrecedesOrEquals(cutoffs->relfrozenxid, xid));
7114		Assert(TransactionIdPrecedes(xid, cutoffs->OldestXmin));
7115
7116		/*
7117		 * For Xvac, we always freeze proactively.  This allows totally_frozen
7118		 * tracking to ignore xvac.
7119		 */
7120		replace_xvac = pagefrz->freeze_required = true;
7121
7122		/* Will set replace_xvac flags in freeze plan below */
7123	}
7124
7125	/* Now process xmax */
7126	xid = frz->xmax;
7127	if (tuple->t_infomask & HEAP_XMAX_IS_MULTI)
7128	{
7129		/* Raw xmax is a MultiXactId */
7130		TransactionId newxmax;
7131		uint16		flags;
7132
7133		/*
7134		 * We will either remove xmax completely (in the "freeze_xmax" path),
7135		 * process xmax by replacing it (in the "replace_xmax" path), or
7136		 * perform no-op xmax processing.  The only constraint is that the
7137		 * FreezeLimit/MultiXactCutoff postcondition must never be violated.
7138		 */
7139		newxmax = FreezeMultiXactId(xid, tuple->t_infomask, cutoffs,
7140									&flags, pagefrz);
7141
7142		if (flags & FRM_NOOP)
7143		{
7144			/*
7145			 * xmax is a MultiXactId, and nothing about it changes for now.
7146			 * This is the only case where 'freeze_required' won't have been
7147			 * set for us by FreezeMultiXactId, as well as the only case where
7148			 * neither freeze_xmax nor replace_xmax are set (given a multi).
7149			 *
7150			 * This is a no-op, but the call to FreezeMultiXactId might have
7151			 * ratcheted back NewRelfrozenXid and/or NewRelminMxid trackers
7152			 * for us (the "freeze page" variants, specifically).  That'll
7153			 * make it safe for our caller to freeze the page later on, while
7154			 * leaving this particular xmax undisturbed.
7155			 *
7156			 * FreezeMultiXactId is _not_ responsible for the "no freeze"
7157			 * NewRelfrozenXid/NewRelminMxid trackers, though -- that's our
7158			 * job.  A call to heap_tuple_should_freeze for this same tuple
7159			 * will take place below if 'freeze_required' isn't set already.
7160			 * (This repeats work from FreezeMultiXactId, but allows "no
7161			 * freeze" tracker maintenance to happen in only one place.)
7162			 */
7163			Assert(!MultiXactIdPrecedes(newxmax, cutoffs->MultiXactCutoff));
7164			Assert(MultiXactIdIsValid(newxmax) && xid == newxmax);
7165		}
7166		else if (flags & FRM_RETURN_IS_XID)
7167		{
7168			/*
7169			 * xmax will become an updater Xid (original MultiXact's updater
7170			 * member Xid will be carried forward as a simple Xid in Xmax).
7171			 */
7172			Assert(!TransactionIdPrecedes(newxmax, cutoffs->OldestXmin));
7173
7174			/*
7175			 * NB -- some of these transformations are only valid because we
7176			 * know the return Xid is a tuple updater (i.e. not merely a
7177			 * locker.) Also note that the only reason we don't explicitly
7178			 * worry about HEAP_KEYS_UPDATED is because it lives in
7179			 * t_infomask2 rather than t_infomask.
7180			 */
7181			frz->t_infomask &= ~HEAP_XMAX_BITS;
7182			frz->xmax = newxmax;
7183			if (flags & FRM_MARK_COMMITTED)
7184				frz->t_infomask |= HEAP_XMAX_COMMITTED;
7185			replace_xmax = true;
7186		}
7187		else if (flags & FRM_RETURN_IS_MULTI)
7188		{
7189			uint16		newbits;
7190			uint16		newbits2;
7191
7192			/*
7193			 * xmax is an old MultiXactId that we have to replace with a new
7194			 * MultiXactId, to carry forward two or more original member XIDs.
7195			 */
7196			Assert(!MultiXactIdPrecedes(newxmax, cutoffs->OldestMxact));
7197
7198			/*
7199			 * We can't use GetMultiXactIdHintBits directly on the new multi
7200			 * here; that routine initializes the masks to all zeroes, which
7201			 * would lose other bits we need.  Doing it this way ensures all
7202			 * unrelated bits remain untouched.
7203			 */
7204			frz->t_infomask &= ~HEAP_XMAX_BITS;
7205			frz->t_infomask2 &= ~HEAP_KEYS_UPDATED;
7206			GetMultiXactIdHintBits(newxmax, &newbits, &newbits2);
7207			frz->t_infomask |= newbits;
7208			frz->t_infomask2 |= newbits2;
7209			frz->xmax = newxmax;
7210			replace_xmax = true;
7211		}
7212		else
7213		{
7214			/*
7215			 * Freeze plan for tuple "freezes xmax" in the strictest sense:
7216			 * it'll leave nothing in xmax (neither an Xid nor a MultiXactId).
7217			 */
7218			Assert(flags & FRM_INVALIDATE_XMAX);
7219			Assert(!TransactionIdIsValid(newxmax));
7220
7221			/* Will set freeze_xmax flags in freeze plan below */
7222			freeze_xmax = true;
7223		}
7224
7225		/* MultiXactId processing forces freezing (barring FRM_NOOP case) */
7226		Assert(pagefrz->freeze_required || (!freeze_xmax && !replace_xmax));
7227	}
7228	else if (TransactionIdIsNormal(xid))
7229	{
7230		/* Raw xmax is normal XID */
7231		if (TransactionIdPrecedes(xid, cutoffs->relfrozenxid))
7232			ereport(ERROR,
7233					(errcode(ERRCODE_DATA_CORRUPTED),
7234					 errmsg_internal("found xmax %u from before relfrozenxid %u",
7235									 xid, cutoffs->relfrozenxid)));
7236
7237		/* Will set freeze_xmax flags in freeze plan below */
7238		freeze_xmax = TransactionIdPrecedes(xid, cutoffs->OldestXmin);
7239
7240		/*
7241		 * Verify that xmax aborted if and when freeze plan is executed,
7242		 * provided it's from an update. (A lock-only xmax can be removed
7243		 * independent of this, since the lock is released at xact end.)
7244		 */
7245		if (freeze_xmax && !HEAP_XMAX_IS_LOCKED_ONLY(tuple->t_infomask))
7246			frz->checkflags |= HEAP_FREEZE_CHECK_XMAX_ABORTED;
7247	}
7248	else if (!TransactionIdIsValid(xid))
7249	{
7250		/* Raw xmax is InvalidTransactionId XID */
7251		Assert((tuple->t_infomask & HEAP_XMAX_IS_MULTI) == 0);
7252		xmax_already_frozen = true;
7253	}
7254	else
7255		ereport(ERROR,
7256				(errcode(ERRCODE_DATA_CORRUPTED),
7257				 errmsg_internal("found raw xmax %u (infomask 0x%04x) not invalid and not multi",
7258								 xid, tuple->t_infomask)));
7259
7260	if (freeze_xmin)
7261	{
7262		Assert(!xmin_already_frozen);
7263
7264		frz->t_infomask |= HEAP_XMIN_FROZEN;
7265	}
7266	if (replace_xvac)
7267	{
7268		/*
7269		 * If a MOVED_OFF tuple is not dead, the xvac transaction must have
7270		 * failed; whereas a non-dead MOVED_IN tuple must mean the xvac
7271		 * transaction succeeded.
7272		 */
7273		Assert(pagefrz->freeze_required);
7274		if (tuple->t_infomask & HEAP_MOVED_OFF)
7275			frz->frzflags |= XLH_INVALID_XVAC;
7276		else
7277			frz->frzflags |= XLH_FREEZE_XVAC;
7278	}
7279	if (replace_xmax)
7280	{
7281		Assert(!xmax_already_frozen && !freeze_xmax);
7282		Assert(pagefrz->freeze_required);
7283
7284		/* Already set replace_xmax flags in freeze plan earlier */
7285	}
7286	if (freeze_xmax)
7287	{
7288		Assert(!xmax_already_frozen && !replace_xmax);
7289
7290		frz->xmax = InvalidTransactionId;
7291
7292		/*
7293		 * The tuple might be marked either XMAX_INVALID or XMAX_COMMITTED +
7294		 * LOCKED.  Normalize to INVALID just to be sure no one gets confused.
7295		 * Also get rid of the HEAP_KEYS_UPDATED bit.
7296		 */
7297		frz->t_infomask &= ~HEAP_XMAX_BITS;
7298		frz->t_infomask |= HEAP_XMAX_INVALID;
7299		frz->t_infomask2 &= ~HEAP_HOT_UPDATED;
7300		frz->t_infomask2 &= ~HEAP_KEYS_UPDATED;
7301	}
7302
7303	/*
7304	 * Determine if this tuple is already totally frozen, or will become
7305	 * totally frozen (provided caller executes freeze plans for the page)
7306	 */
7307	*totally_frozen = ((freeze_xmin || xmin_already_frozen) &&
7308					   (freeze_xmax || xmax_already_frozen));
7309
7310	if (!pagefrz->freeze_required && !(xmin_already_frozen &&
7311									   xmax_already_frozen))
7312	{
7313		/*
7314		 * So far no previous tuple from the page made freezing mandatory.
7315		 * Does this tuple force caller to freeze the entire page?
7316		 */
7317		pagefrz->freeze_required =
7318			heap_tuple_should_freeze(tuple, cutoffs,
7319									 &pagefrz->NoFreezePageRelfrozenXid,
7320									 &pagefrz->NoFreezePageRelminMxid);
7321	}
7322
7323	/* Tell caller if this tuple has a usable freeze plan set in *frz */
7324	return freeze_xmin || replace_xvac || replace_xmax || freeze_xmax;
7325}
7326
7327/*
7328 * Perform xmin/xmax XID status sanity checks before actually executing freeze
7329 * plans.
7330 *
7331 * heap_prepare_freeze_tuple doesn't perform these checks directly because
7332 * pg_xact lookups are relatively expensive.  They shouldn't be repeated by
7333 * successive VACUUMs that each decide against freezing the same page.
7334 */
7335void
7336heap_pre_freeze_checks(Buffer buffer,
7337					   HeapTupleFreeze *tuples, int ntuples)
7338{
7339	Page		page = BufferGetPage(buffer);
7340
7341	for (int i = 0; i < ntuples; i++)
7342	{
7343		HeapTupleFreeze *frz = tuples + i;
7344		ItemId		itemid = PageGetItemId(page, frz->offset);
7345		HeapTupleHeader htup;
7346
7347		htup = (HeapTupleHeader) PageGetItem(page, itemid);
7348
7349		/* Deliberately avoid relying on tuple hint bits here */
7350		if (frz->checkflags & HEAP_FREEZE_CHECK_XMIN_COMMITTED)
7351		{
7352			TransactionId xmin = HeapTupleHeaderGetRawXmin(htup);
7353
7354			Assert(!HeapTupleHeaderXminFrozen(htup));
7355			if (unlikely(!TransactionIdDidCommit(xmin)))
7356				ereport(ERROR,
7357						(errcode(ERRCODE_DATA_CORRUPTED),
7358						 errmsg_internal("uncommitted xmin %u needs to be frozen",
7359										 xmin)));
7360		}
7361
7362		/*
7363		 * TransactionIdDidAbort won't work reliably in the presence of XIDs
7364		 * left behind by transactions that were in progress during a crash,
7365		 * so we can only check that xmax didn't commit
7366		 */
7367		if (frz->checkflags & HEAP_FREEZE_CHECK_XMAX_ABORTED)
7368		{
7369			TransactionId xmax = HeapTupleHeaderGetRawXmax(htup);
7370
7371			Assert(TransactionIdIsNormal(xmax));
7372			if (unlikely(TransactionIdDidCommit(xmax)))
7373				ereport(ERROR,
7374						(errcode(ERRCODE_DATA_CORRUPTED),
7375						 errmsg_internal("cannot freeze committed xmax %u",
7376										 xmax)));
7377		}
7378	}
7379}
7380
7381/*
7382 * Helper which executes freezing of one or more heap tuples on a page on
7383 * behalf of caller.  Caller passes an array of tuple plans from
7384 * heap_prepare_freeze_tuple.  Caller must set 'offset' in each plan for us.
7385 * Must be called in a critical section that also marks the buffer dirty and,
7386 * if needed, emits WAL.
7387 */
7388void
7389heap_freeze_prepared_tuples(Buffer buffer, HeapTupleFreeze *tuples, int ntuples)
7390{
7391	Page		page = BufferGetPage(buffer);
7392
7393	for (int i = 0; i < ntuples; i++)
7394	{
7395		HeapTupleFreeze *frz = tuples + i;
7396		ItemId		itemid = PageGetItemId(page, frz->offset);
7397		HeapTupleHeader htup;
7398
7399		htup = (HeapTupleHeader) PageGetItem(page, itemid);
7400		heap_execute_freeze_tuple(htup, frz);
7401	}
7402}
7403
7404/*
7405 * heap_freeze_tuple
7406 *		Freeze tuple in place, without WAL logging.
7407 *
7408 * Useful for callers like CLUSTER that perform their own WAL logging.
7409 */
7410bool
7411heap_freeze_tuple(HeapTupleHeader tuple,
7412				  TransactionId relfrozenxid, TransactionId relminmxid,
7413				  TransactionId FreezeLimit, TransactionId MultiXactCutoff)
7414{
7415	HeapTupleFreeze frz;
7416	bool		do_freeze;
7417	bool		totally_frozen;
7418	struct VacuumCutoffs cutoffs;
7419	HeapPageFreeze pagefrz;
7420
7421	cutoffs.relfrozenxid = relfrozenxid;
7422	cutoffs.relminmxid = relminmxid;
7423	cutoffs.OldestXmin = FreezeLimit;
7424	cutoffs.OldestMxact = MultiXactCutoff;
7425	cutoffs.FreezeLimit = FreezeLimit;
7426	cutoffs.MultiXactCutoff = MultiXactCutoff;
7427
7428	pagefrz.freeze_required = true;
7429	pagefrz.FreezePageRelfrozenXid = FreezeLimit;
7430	pagefrz.FreezePageRelminMxid = MultiXactCutoff;
7431	pagefrz.NoFreezePageRelfrozenXid = FreezeLimit;
7432	pagefrz.NoFreezePageRelminMxid = MultiXactCutoff;
7433
7434	do_freeze = heap_prepare_freeze_tuple(tuple, &cutoffs,
7435										  &pagefrz, &frz, &totally_frozen);
7436
7437	/*
7438	 * Note that because this is not a WAL-logged operation, we don't need to
7439	 * fill in the offset in the freeze record.
7440	 */
7441
7442	if (do_freeze)
7443		heap_execute_freeze_tuple(tuple, &frz);
7444	return do_freeze;
7445}
7446
7447/*
7448 * For a given MultiXactId, return the hint bits that should be set in the
7449 * tuple's infomask.
7450 *
7451 * Normally this should be called for a multixact that was just created, and
7452 * so is on our local cache, so the GetMembers call is fast.
7453 */
7454static void
7455GetMultiXactIdHintBits(MultiXactId multi, uint16 *new_infomask,
7456					   uint16 *new_infomask2)
7457{
7458	int			nmembers;
7459	MultiXactMember *members;
7460	int			i;
7461	uint16		bits = HEAP_XMAX_IS_MULTI;
7462	uint16		bits2 = 0;
7463	bool		has_update = false;
7464	LockTupleMode strongest = LockTupleKeyShare;
7465
7466	/*
7467	 * We only use this in multis we just created, so they cannot be values
7468	 * pre-pg_upgrade.
7469	 */
7470	nmembers = GetMultiXactIdMembers(multi, &members, false, false);
7471
7472	for (i = 0; i < nmembers; i++)
7473	{
7474		LockTupleMode mode;
7475
7476		/*
7477		 * Remember the strongest lock mode held by any member of the
7478		 * multixact.
7479		 */
7480		mode = TUPLOCK_from_mxstatus(members[i].status);
7481		if (mode > strongest)
7482			strongest = mode;
7483
7484		/* See what other bits we need */
7485		switch (members[i].status)
7486		{
7487			case MultiXactStatusForKeyShare:
7488			case MultiXactStatusForShare:
7489			case MultiXactStatusForNoKeyUpdate:
7490				break;
7491
7492			case MultiXactStatusForUpdate:
7493				bits2 |= HEAP_KEYS_UPDATED;
7494				break;
7495
7496			case MultiXactStatusNoKeyUpdate:
7497				has_update = true;
7498				break;
7499
7500			case MultiXactStatusUpdate:
7501				bits2 |= HEAP_KEYS_UPDATED;
7502				has_update = true;
7503				break;
7504		}
7505	}
7506
7507	if (strongest == LockTupleExclusive ||
7508		strongest == LockTupleNoKeyExclusive)
7509		bits |= HEAP_XMAX_EXCL_LOCK;
7510	else if (strongest == LockTupleShare)
7511		bits |= HEAP_XMAX_SHR_LOCK;
7512	else if (strongest == LockTupleKeyShare)
7513		bits |= HEAP_XMAX_KEYSHR_LOCK;
7514
7515	if (!has_update)
7516		bits |= HEAP_XMAX_LOCK_ONLY;
7517
7518	if (nmembers > 0)
7519		pfree(members);
7520
7521	*new_infomask = bits;
7522	*new_infomask2 = bits2;
7523}
7524
7525/*
7526 * MultiXactIdGetUpdateXid
7527 *
7528 * Given a multixact Xmax and corresponding infomask, which does not have the
7529 * HEAP_XMAX_LOCK_ONLY bit set, obtain and return the Xid of the updating
7530 * transaction.
7531 *
7532 * Caller is expected to check the status of the updating transaction, if
7533 * necessary.
7534 */
7535static TransactionId
7536MultiXactIdGetUpdateXid(TransactionId xmax, uint16 t_infomask)
7537{
7538	TransactionId update_xact = InvalidTransactionId;
7539	MultiXactMember *members;
7540	int			nmembers;
7541
7542	Assert(!(t_infomask & HEAP_XMAX_LOCK_ONLY));
7543	Assert(t_infomask & HEAP_XMAX_IS_MULTI);
7544
7545	/*
7546	 * Since we know the LOCK_ONLY bit is not set, this cannot be a multi from
7547	 * pre-pg_upgrade.
7548	 */
7549	nmembers = GetMultiXactIdMembers(xmax, &members, false, false);
7550
7551	if (nmembers > 0)
7552	{
7553		int			i;
7554
7555		for (i = 0; i < nmembers; i++)
7556		{
7557			/* Ignore lockers */
7558			if (!ISUPDATE_from_mxstatus(members[i].status))
7559				continue;
7560
7561			/* there can be at most one updater */
7562			Assert(update_xact == InvalidTransactionId);
7563			update_xact = members[i].xid;
7564#ifndef USE_ASSERT_CHECKING
7565
7566			/*
7567			 * in an assert-enabled build, walk the whole array to ensure
7568			 * there's no other updater.
7569			 */
7570			break;
7571#endif
7572		}
7573
7574		pfree(members);
7575	}
7576
7577	return update_xact;
7578}
7579
7580/*
7581 * HeapTupleGetUpdateXid
7582 *		As above, but use a HeapTupleHeader
7583 *
7584 * See also HeapTupleHeaderGetUpdateXid, which can be used without previously
7585 * checking the hint bits.
7586 */
7587TransactionId
7588HeapTupleGetUpdateXid(const HeapTupleHeaderData *tup)
7589{
7590	return MultiXactIdGetUpdateXid(HeapTupleHeaderGetRawXmax(tup),
7591								   tup->t_infomask);
7592}
7593
7594/*
7595 * Does the given multixact conflict with the current transaction grabbing a
7596 * tuple lock of the given strength?
7597 *
7598 * The passed infomask pairs up with the given multixact in the tuple header.
7599 *
7600 * If current_is_member is not NULL, it is set to 'true' if the current
7601 * transaction is a member of the given multixact.
7602 */
7603static bool
7604DoesMultiXactIdConflict(MultiXactId multi, uint16 infomask,
7605						LockTupleMode lockmode, bool *current_is_member)
7606{
7607	int			nmembers;
7608	MultiXactMember *members;
7609	bool		result = false;
7610	LOCKMODE	wanted = tupleLockExtraInfo[lockmode].hwlock;
7611
7612	if (HEAP_LOCKED_UPGRADED(infomask))
7613		return false;
7614
7615	nmembers = GetMultiXactIdMembers(multi, &members, false,
7616									 HEAP_XMAX_IS_LOCKED_ONLY(infomask));
7617	if (nmembers >= 0)
7618	{
7619		int			i;
7620
7621		for (i = 0; i < nmembers; i++)
7622		{
7623			TransactionId memxid;
7624			LOCKMODE	memlockmode;
7625
7626			if (result && (current_is_member == NULL || *current_is_member))
7627				break;
7628
7629			memlockmode = LOCKMODE_from_mxstatus(members[i].status);
7630
7631			/* ignore members from current xact (but track their presence) */
7632			memxid = members[i].xid;
7633			if (TransactionIdIsCurrentTransactionId(memxid))
7634			{
7635				if (current_is_member != NULL)
7636					*current_is_member = true;
7637				continue;
7638			}
7639			else if (result)
7640				continue;
7641
7642			/* ignore members that don't conflict with the lock we want */
7643			if (!DoLockModesConflict(memlockmode, wanted))
7644				continue;
7645
7646			if (ISUPDATE_from_mxstatus(members[i].status))
7647			{
7648				/* ignore aborted updaters */
7649				if (TransactionIdDidAbort(memxid))
7650					continue;
7651			}
7652			else
7653			{
7654				/* ignore lockers-only that are no longer in progress */
7655				if (!TransactionIdIsInProgress(memxid))
7656					continue;
7657			}
7658
7659			/*
7660			 * Whatever remains are either live lockers that conflict with our
7661			 * wanted lock, and updaters that are not aborted.  Those conflict
7662			 * with what we want.  Set up to return true, but keep going to
7663			 * look for the current transaction among the multixact members,
7664			 * if needed.
7665			 */
7666			result = true;
7667		}
7668		pfree(members);
7669	}
7670
7671	return result;
7672}
7673
7674/*
7675 * Do_MultiXactIdWait
7676 *		Actual implementation for the two functions below.
7677 *
7678 * 'multi', 'status' and 'infomask' indicate what to sleep on (the status is
7679 * needed to ensure we only sleep on conflicting members, and the infomask is
7680 * used to optimize multixact access in case it's a lock-only multi); 'nowait'
7681 * indicates whether to use conditional lock acquisition, to allow callers to
7682 * fail if lock is unavailable.  'rel', 'ctid' and 'oper' are used to set up
7683 * context information for error messages.  'remaining', if not NULL, receives
7684 * the number of members that are still running, including any (non-aborted)
7685 * subtransactions of our own transaction.  'logLockFailure' indicates whether
7686 * to log details when a lock acquisition fails with 'nowait' enabled.
7687 *
7688 * We do this by sleeping on each member using XactLockTableWait.  Any
7689 * members that belong to the current backend are *not* waited for, however;
7690 * this would not merely be useless but would lead to Assert failure inside
7691 * XactLockTableWait.  By the time this returns, it is certain that all
7692 * transactions *of other backends* that were members of the MultiXactId
7693 * that conflict with the requested status are dead (and no new ones can have
7694 * been added, since it is not legal to add members to an existing
7695 * MultiXactId).
7696 *
7697 * But by the time we finish sleeping, someone else may have changed the Xmax
7698 * of the containing tuple, so the caller needs to iterate on us somehow.
7699 *
7700 * Note that in case we return false, the number of remaining members is
7701 * not to be trusted.
7702 */
7703static bool
7704Do_MultiXactIdWait(MultiXactId multi, MultiXactStatus status,
7705				   uint16 infomask, bool nowait,
7706				   Relation rel, ItemPointer ctid, XLTW_Oper oper,
7707				   int *remaining, bool logLockFailure)
7708{
7709	bool		result = true;
7710	MultiXactMember *members;
7711	int			nmembers;
7712	int			remain = 0;
7713
7714	/* for pre-pg_upgrade tuples, no need to sleep at all */
7715	nmembers = HEAP_LOCKED_UPGRADED(infomask) ? -1 :
7716		GetMultiXactIdMembers(multi, &members, false,
7717							  HEAP_XMAX_IS_LOCKED_ONLY(infomask));
7718
7719	if (nmembers >= 0)
7720	{
7721		int			i;
7722
7723		for (i = 0; i < nmembers; i++)
7724		{
7725			TransactionId memxid = members[i].xid;
7726			MultiXactStatus memstatus = members[i].status;
7727
7728			if (TransactionIdIsCurrentTransactionId(memxid))
7729			{
7730				remain++;
7731				continue;
7732			}
7733
7734			if (!DoLockModesConflict(LOCKMODE_from_mxstatus(memstatus),
7735									 LOCKMODE_from_mxstatus(status)))
7736			{
7737				if (remaining && TransactionIdIsInProgress(memxid))
7738					remain++;
7739				continue;
7740			}
7741
7742			/*
7743			 * This member conflicts with our multi, so we have to sleep (or
7744			 * return failure, if asked to avoid waiting.)
7745			 *
7746			 * Note that we don't set up an error context callback ourselves,
7747			 * but instead we pass the info down to XactLockTableWait.  This
7748			 * might seem a bit wasteful because the context is set up and
7749			 * tore down for each member of the multixact, but in reality it
7750			 * should be barely noticeable, and it avoids duplicate code.
7751			 */
7752			if (nowait)
7753			{
7754				result = ConditionalXactLockTableWait(memxid, logLockFailure);
7755				if (!result)
7756					break;
7757			}
7758			else
7759				XactLockTableWait(memxid, rel, ctid, oper);
7760		}
7761
7762		pfree(members);
7763	}
7764
7765	if (remaining)
7766		*remaining = remain;
7767
7768	return result;
7769}
7770
7771/*
7772 * MultiXactIdWait
7773 *		Sleep on a MultiXactId.
7774 *
7775 * By the time we finish sleeping, someone else may have changed the Xmax
7776 * of the containing tuple, so the caller needs to iterate on us somehow.
7777 *
7778 * We return (in *remaining, if not NULL) the number of members that are still
7779 * running, including any (non-aborted) subtransactions of our own transaction.
7780 */
7781static void
7782MultiXactIdWait(MultiXactId multi, MultiXactStatus status, uint16 infomask,
7783				Relation rel, ItemPointer ctid, XLTW_Oper oper,
7784				int *remaining)
7785{
7786	(void) Do_MultiXactIdWait(multi, status, infomask, false,
7787							  rel, ctid, oper, remaining, false);
7788}
7789
7790/*
7791 * ConditionalMultiXactIdWait
7792 *		As above, but only lock if we can get the lock without blocking.
7793 *
7794 * By the time we finish sleeping, someone else may have changed the Xmax
7795 * of the containing tuple, so the caller needs to iterate on us somehow.
7796 *
7797 * If the multixact is now all gone, return true.  Returns false if some
7798 * transactions might still be running.
7799 *
7800 * We return (in *remaining, if not NULL) the number of members that are still
7801 * running, including any (non-aborted) subtransactions of our own transaction.
7802 */
7803static bool
7804ConditionalMultiXactIdWait(MultiXactId multi, MultiXactStatus status,
7805						   uint16 infomask, Relation rel, int *remaining,
7806						   bool logLockFailure)
7807{
7808	return Do_MultiXactIdWait(multi, status, infomask, true,
7809							  rel, NULL, XLTW_None, remaining, logLockFailure);
7810}
7811
7812/*
7813 * heap_tuple_needs_eventual_freeze
7814 *
7815 * Check to see whether any of the XID fields of a tuple (xmin, xmax, xvac)
7816 * will eventually require freezing (if tuple isn't removed by pruning first).
7817 */
7818bool
7819heap_tuple_needs_eventual_freeze(HeapTupleHeader tuple)
7820{
7821	TransactionId xid;
7822
7823	/*
7824	 * If xmin is a normal transaction ID, this tuple is definitely not
7825	 * frozen.
7826	 */
7827	xid = HeapTupleHeaderGetXmin(tuple);
7828	if (TransactionIdIsNormal(xid))
7829		return true;
7830
7831	/*
7832	 * If xmax is a valid xact or multixact, this tuple is also not frozen.
7833	 */
7834	if (tuple->t_infomask & HEAP_XMAX_IS_MULTI)
7835	{
7836		MultiXactId multi;
7837
7838		multi = HeapTupleHeaderGetRawXmax(tuple);
7839		if (MultiXactIdIsValid(multi))
7840			return true;
7841	}
7842	else
7843	{
7844		xid = HeapTupleHeaderGetRawXmax(tuple);
7845		if (TransactionIdIsNormal(xid))
7846			return true;
7847	}
7848
7849	if (tuple->t_infomask & HEAP_MOVED)
7850	{
7851		xid = HeapTupleHeaderGetXvac(tuple);
7852		if (TransactionIdIsNormal(xid))
7853			return true;
7854	}
7855
7856	return false;
7857}
7858
7859/*
7860 * heap_tuple_should_freeze
7861 *
7862 * Return value indicates if heap_prepare_freeze_tuple sibling function would
7863 * (or should) force freezing of the heap page that contains caller's tuple.
7864 * Tuple header XIDs/MXIDs < FreezeLimit/MultiXactCutoff trigger freezing.
7865 * This includes (xmin, xmax, xvac) fields, as well as MultiXact member XIDs.
7866 *
7867 * The *NoFreezePageRelfrozenXid and *NoFreezePageRelminMxid input/output
7868 * arguments help VACUUM track the oldest extant XID/MXID remaining in rel.
7869 * Our working assumption is that caller won't decide to freeze this tuple.
7870 * It's up to caller to only ratchet back its own top-level trackers after the
7871 * point that it fully commits to not freezing the tuple/page in question.
7872 */
7873bool
7874heap_tuple_should_freeze(HeapTupleHeader tuple,
7875						 const struct VacuumCutoffs *cutoffs,
7876						 TransactionId *NoFreezePageRelfrozenXid,
7877						 MultiXactId *NoFreezePageRelminMxid)
7878{
7879	TransactionId xid;
7880	MultiXactId multi;
7881	bool		freeze = false;
7882
7883	/* First deal with xmin */
7884	xid = HeapTupleHeaderGetXmin(tuple);
7885	if (TransactionIdIsNormal(xid))
7886	{
7887		Assert(TransactionIdPrecedesOrEquals(cutoffs->relfrozenxid, xid));
7888		if (TransactionIdPrecedes(xid, *NoFreezePageRelfrozenXid))
7889			*NoFreezePageRelfrozenXid = xid;
7890		if (TransactionIdPrecedes(xid, cutoffs->FreezeLimit))
7891			freeze = true;
7892	}
7893
7894	/* Now deal with xmax */
7895	xid = InvalidTransactionId;
7896	multi = InvalidMultiXactId;
7897	if (tuple->t_infomask & HEAP_XMAX_IS_MULTI)
7898		multi = HeapTupleHeaderGetRawXmax(tuple);
7899	else
7900		xid = HeapTupleHeaderGetRawXmax(tuple);
7901
7902	if (TransactionIdIsNormal(xid))
7903	{
7904		Assert(TransactionIdPrecedesOrEquals(cutoffs->relfrozenxid, xid));
7905		/* xmax is a non-permanent XID */
7906		if (TransactionIdPrecedes(xid, *NoFreezePageRelfrozenXid))
7907			*NoFreezePageRelfrozenXid = xid;
7908		if (TransactionIdPrecedes(xid, cutoffs->FreezeLimit))
7909			freeze = true;
7910	}
7911	else if (!MultiXactIdIsValid(multi))
7912	{
7913		/* xmax is a permanent XID or invalid MultiXactId/XID */
7914	}
7915	else if (HEAP_LOCKED_UPGRADED(tuple->t_infomask))
7916	{
7917		/* xmax is a pg_upgrade'd MultiXact, which can't have updater XID */
7918		if (MultiXactIdPrecedes(multi, *NoFreezePageRelminMxid))
7919			*NoFreezePageRelminMxid = multi;
7920		/* heap_prepare_freeze_tuple always freezes pg_upgrade'd xmax */
7921		freeze = true;
7922	}
7923	else
7924	{
7925		/* xmax is a MultiXactId that may have an updater XID */
7926		MultiXactMember *members;
7927		int			nmembers;
7928
7929		Assert(MultiXactIdPrecedesOrEquals(cutoffs->relminmxid, multi));
7930		if (MultiXactIdPrecedes(multi, *NoFreezePageRelminMxid))
7931			*NoFreezePageRelminMxid = multi;
7932		if (MultiXactIdPrecedes(multi, cutoffs->MultiXactCutoff))
7933			freeze = true;
7934
7935		/* need to check whether any member of the mxact is old */
7936		nmembers = GetMultiXactIdMembers(multi, &members, false,
7937										 HEAP_XMAX_IS_LOCKED_ONLY(tuple->t_infomask));
7938
7939		for (int i = 0; i < nmembers; i++)
7940		{
7941			xid = members[i].xid;
7942			Assert(TransactionIdPrecedesOrEquals(cutoffs->relfrozenxid, xid));
7943			if (TransactionIdPrecedes(xid, *NoFreezePageRelfrozenXid))
7944				*NoFreezePageRelfrozenXid = xid;
7945			if (TransactionIdPrecedes(xid, cutoffs->FreezeLimit))
7946				freeze = true;
7947		}
7948		if (nmembers > 0)
7949			pfree(members);
7950	}
7951
7952	if (tuple->t_infomask & HEAP_MOVED)
7953	{
7954		xid = HeapTupleHeaderGetXvac(tuple);
7955		if (TransactionIdIsNormal(xid))
7956		{
7957			Assert(TransactionIdPrecedesOrEquals(cutoffs->relfrozenxid, xid));
7958			if (TransactionIdPrecedes(xid, *NoFreezePageRelfrozenXid))
7959				*NoFreezePageRelfrozenXid = xid;
7960			/* heap_prepare_freeze_tuple forces xvac freezing */
7961			freeze = true;
7962		}
7963	}
7964
7965	return freeze;
7966}
7967
7968/*
7969 * Maintain snapshotConflictHorizon for caller by ratcheting forward its value
7970 * using any committed XIDs contained in 'tuple', an obsolescent heap tuple
7971 * that caller is in the process of physically removing, e.g. via HOT pruning
7972 * or index deletion.
7973 *
7974 * Caller must initialize its value to InvalidTransactionId, which is
7975 * generally interpreted as "definitely no need for a recovery conflict".
7976 * Final value must reflect all heap tuples that caller will physically remove
7977 * (or remove TID references to) via its ongoing pruning/deletion operation.
7978 * ResolveRecoveryConflictWithSnapshot() is passed the final value (taken from
7979 * caller's WAL record) by REDO routine when it replays caller's operation.
7980 */
7981void
7982HeapTupleHeaderAdvanceConflictHorizon(HeapTupleHeader tuple,
7983									  TransactionId *snapshotConflictHorizon)
7984{
7985	TransactionId xmin = HeapTupleHeaderGetXmin(tuple);
7986	TransactionId xmax = HeapTupleHeaderGetUpdateXid(tuple);
7987	TransactionId xvac = HeapTupleHeaderGetXvac(tuple);
7988
7989	if (tuple->t_infomask & HEAP_MOVED)
7990	{
7991		if (TransactionIdPrecedes(*snapshotConflictHorizon, xvac))
7992			*snapshotConflictHorizon = xvac;
7993	}
7994
7995	/*
7996	 * Ignore tuples inserted by an aborted transaction or if the tuple was
7997	 * updated/deleted by the inserting transaction.
7998	 *
7999	 * Look for a committed hint bit, or if no xmin bit is set, check clog.
8000	 */
8001	if (HeapTupleHeaderXminCommitted(tuple) ||
8002		(!HeapTupleHeaderXminInvalid(tuple) && TransactionIdDidCommit(xmin)))
8003	{
8004		if (xmax != xmin &&
8005			TransactionIdFollows(xmax, *snapshotConflictHorizon))
8006			*snapshotConflictHorizon = xmax;
8007	}
8008}
8009
8010#ifdef USE_PREFETCH
8011/*
8012 * Helper function for heap_index_delete_tuples.  Issues prefetch requests for
8013 * prefetch_count buffers.  The prefetch_state keeps track of all the buffers
8014 * we can prefetch, and which have already been prefetched; each call to this
8015 * function picks up where the previous call left off.
8016 *
8017 * Note: we expect the deltids array to be sorted in an order that groups TIDs
8018 * by heap block, with all TIDs for each block appearing together in exactly
8019 * one group.
8020 */
8021static void
8022index_delete_prefetch_buffer(Relation rel,
8023							 IndexDeletePrefetchState *prefetch_state,
8024							 int prefetch_count)
8025{
8026	BlockNumber cur_hblkno = prefetch_state->cur_hblkno;
8027	int			count = 0;
8028	int			i;
8029	int			ndeltids = prefetch_state->ndeltids;
8030	TM_IndexDelete *deltids = prefetch_state->deltids;
8031
8032	for (i = prefetch_state->next_item;
8033		 i < ndeltids && count < prefetch_count;
8034		 i++)
8035	{
8036		ItemPointer htid = &deltids[i].tid;
8037
8038		if (cur_hblkno == InvalidBlockNumber ||
8039			ItemPointerGetBlockNumber(htid) != cur_hblkno)
8040		{
8041			cur_hblkno = ItemPointerGetBlockNumber(htid);
8042			PrefetchBuffer(rel, MAIN_FORKNUM, cur_hblkno);
8043			count++;
8044		}
8045	}
8046
8047	/*
8048	 * Save the prefetch position so that next time we can continue from that
8049	 * position.
8050	 */
8051	prefetch_state->next_item = i;
8052	prefetch_state->cur_hblkno = cur_hblkno;
8053}
8054#endif
8055
8056/*
8057 * Helper function for heap_index_delete_tuples.  Checks for index corruption
8058 * involving an invalid TID in index AM caller's index page.
8059 *
8060 * This is an ideal place for these checks.  The index AM must hold a buffer
8061 * lock on the index page containing the TIDs we examine here, so we don't
8062 * have to worry about concurrent VACUUMs at all.  We can be sure that the
8063 * index is corrupt when htid points directly to an LP_UNUSED item or
8064 * heap-only tuple, which is not the case during standard index scans.
8065 */
8066static inline void
8067index_delete_check_htid(TM_IndexDeleteOp *delstate,
8068						Page page, OffsetNumber maxoff,
8069						ItemPointer htid, TM_IndexStatus *istatus)
8070{
8071	OffsetNumber indexpagehoffnum = ItemPointerGetOffsetNumber(htid);
8072	ItemId		iid;
8073
8074	Assert(OffsetNumberIsValid(istatus->idxoffnum));
8075
8076	if (unlikely(indexpagehoffnum > maxoff))
8077		ereport(ERROR,
8078				(errcode(ERRCODE_INDEX_CORRUPTED),
8079				 errmsg_internal("heap tid from index tuple (%u,%u) points past end of heap page line pointer array at offset %u of block %u in index \"%s\"",
8080								 ItemPointerGetBlockNumber(htid),
8081								 indexpagehoffnum,
8082								 istatus->idxoffnum, delstate->iblknum,
8083								 RelationGetRelationName(delstate->irel))));
8084
8085	iid = PageGetItemId(page, indexpagehoffnum);
8086	if (unlikely(!ItemIdIsUsed(iid)))
8087		ereport(ERROR,
8088				(errcode(ERRCODE_INDEX_CORRUPTED),
8089				 errmsg_internal("heap tid from index tuple (%u,%u) points to unused heap page item at offset %u of block %u in index \"%s\"",
8090								 ItemPointerGetBlockNumber(htid),
8091								 indexpagehoffnum,
8092								 istatus->idxoffnum, delstate->iblknum,
8093								 RelationGetRelationName(delstate->irel))));
8094
8095	if (ItemIdHasStorage(iid))
8096	{
8097		HeapTupleHeader htup;
8098
8099		Assert(ItemIdIsNormal(iid));
8100		htup = (HeapTupleHeader) PageGetItem(page, iid);
8101
8102		if (unlikely(HeapTupleHeaderIsHeapOnly(htup)))
8103			ereport(ERROR,
8104					(errcode(ERRCODE_INDEX_CORRUPTED),
8105					 errmsg_internal("heap tid from index tuple (%u,%u) points to heap-only tuple at offset %u of block %u in index \"%s\"",
8106									 ItemPointerGetBlockNumber(htid),
8107									 indexpagehoffnum,
8108									 istatus->idxoffnum, delstate->iblknum,
8109									 RelationGetRelationName(delstate->irel))));
8110	}
8111}
8112
8113/*
8114 * heapam implementation of tableam's index_delete_tuples interface.
8115 *
8116 * This helper function is called by index AMs during index tuple deletion.
8117 * See tableam header comments for an explanation of the interface implemented
8118 * here and a general theory of operation.  Note that each call here is either
8119 * a simple index deletion call, or a bottom-up index deletion call.
8120 *
8121 * It's possible for this to generate a fair amount of I/O, since we may be
8122 * deleting hundreds of tuples from a single index block.  To amortize that
8123 * cost to some degree, this uses prefetching and combines repeat accesses to
8124 * the same heap block.
8125 */
8126TransactionId
8127heap_index_delete_tuples(Relation rel, TM_IndexDeleteOp *delstate)
8128{
8129	/* Initial assumption is that earlier pruning took care of conflict */
8130	TransactionId snapshotConflictHorizon = InvalidTransactionId;
8131	BlockNumber blkno = InvalidBlockNumber;
8132	Buffer		buf = InvalidBuffer;
8133	Page		page = NULL;
8134	OffsetNumber maxoff = InvalidOffsetNumber;
8135	TransactionId priorXmax;
8136#ifdef USE_PREFETCH
8137	IndexDeletePrefetchState prefetch_state;
8138	int			prefetch_distance;
8139#endif
8140	SnapshotData SnapshotNonVacuumable;
8141	int			finalndeltids = 0,
8142				nblocksaccessed = 0;
8143
8144	/* State that's only used in bottom-up index deletion case */
8145	int			nblocksfavorable = 0;
8146	int			curtargetfreespace = delstate->bottomupfreespace,
8147				lastfreespace = 0,
8148				actualfreespace = 0;
8149	bool		bottomup_final_block = false;
8150
8151	InitNonVacuumableSnapshot(SnapshotNonVacuumable, GlobalVisTestFor(rel));
8152
8153	/* Sort caller's deltids array by TID for further processing */
8154	index_delete_sort(delstate);
8155
8156	/*
8157	 * Bottom-up case: resort deltids array in an order attuned to where the
8158	 * greatest number of promising TIDs are to be found, and determine how
8159	 * many blocks from the start of sorted array should be considered
8160	 * favorable.  This will also shrink the deltids array in order to
8161	 * eliminate completely unfavorable blocks up front.
8162	 */
8163	if (delstate->bottomup)
8164		nblocksfavorable = bottomup_sort_and_shrink(delstate);
8165
8166#ifdef USE_PREFETCH
8167	/* Initialize prefetch state. */
8168	prefetch_state.cur_hblkno = InvalidBlockNumber;
8169	prefetch_state.next_item = 0;
8170	prefetch_state.ndeltids = delstate->ndeltids;
8171	prefetch_state.deltids = delstate->deltids;
8172
8173	/*
8174	 * Determine the prefetch distance that we will attempt to maintain.
8175	 *
8176	 * Since the caller holds a buffer lock somewhere in rel, we'd better make
8177	 * sure that isn't a catalog relation before we call code that does
8178	 * syscache lookups, to avoid risk of deadlock.
8179	 */
8180	if (IsCatalogRelation(rel))
8181		prefetch_distance = maintenance_io_concurrency;
8182	else
8183		prefetch_distance =
8184			get_tablespace_maintenance_io_concurrency(rel->rd_rel->reltablespace);
8185
8186	/* Cap initial prefetch distance for bottom-up deletion caller */
8187	if (delstate->bottomup)
8188	{
8189		Assert(nblocksfavorable >= 1);
8190		Assert(nblocksfavorable <= BOTTOMUP_MAX_NBLOCKS);
8191		prefetch_distance = Min(prefetch_distance, nblocksfavorable);
8192	}
8193
8194	/* Start prefetching. */
8195	index_delete_prefetch_buffer(rel, &prefetch_state, prefetch_distance);
8196#endif
8197
8198	/* Iterate over deltids, determine which to delete, check their horizon */
8199	Assert(delstate->ndeltids > 0);
8200	for (int i = 0; i < delstate->ndeltids; i++)
8201	{
8202		TM_IndexDelete *ideltid = &delstate->deltids[i];
8203		TM_IndexStatus *istatus = delstate->status + ideltid->id;
8204		ItemPointer htid = &ideltid->tid;
8205		OffsetNumber offnum;
8206
8207		/*
8208		 * Read buffer, and perform required extra steps each time a new block
8209		 * is encountered.  Avoid refetching if it's the same block as the one
8210		 * from the last htid.
8211		 */
8212		if (blkno == InvalidBlockNumber ||
8213			ItemPointerGetBlockNumber(htid) != blkno)
8214		{
8215			/*
8216			 * Consider giving up early for bottom-up index deletion caller
8217			 * first. (Only prefetch next-next block afterwards, when it
8218			 * becomes clear that we're at least going to access the next
8219			 * block in line.)
8220			 *
8221			 * Sometimes the first block frees so much space for bottom-up
8222			 * caller that the deletion process can end without accessing any
8223			 * more blocks.  It is usually necessary to access 2 or 3 blocks
8224			 * per bottom-up deletion operation, though.
8225			 */
8226			if (delstate->bottomup)
8227			{
8228				/*
8229				 * We often allow caller to delete a few additional items
8230				 * whose entries we reached after the point that space target
8231				 * from caller was satisfied.  The cost of accessing the page
8232				 * was already paid at that point, so it made sense to finish
8233				 * it off.  When that happened, we finalize everything here
8234				 * (by finishing off the whole bottom-up deletion operation
8235				 * without needlessly paying the cost of accessing any more
8236				 * blocks).
8237				 */
8238				if (bottomup_final_block)
8239					break;
8240
8241				/*
8242				 * Give up when we didn't enable our caller to free any
8243				 * additional space as a result of processing the page that we
8244				 * just finished up with.  This rule is the main way in which
8245				 * we keep the cost of bottom-up deletion under control.
8246				 */
8247				if (nblocksaccessed >= 1 && actualfreespace == lastfreespace)
8248					break;
8249				lastfreespace = actualfreespace;	/* for next time */
8250
8251				/*
8252				 * Deletion operation (which is bottom-up) will definitely
8253				 * access the next block in line.  Prepare for that now.
8254				 *
8255				 * Decay target free space so that we don't hang on for too
8256				 * long with a marginal case. (Space target is only truly
8257				 * helpful when it allows us to recognize that we don't need
8258				 * to access more than 1 or 2 blocks to satisfy caller due to
8259				 * agreeable workload characteristics.)
8260				 *
8261				 * We are a bit more patient when we encounter contiguous
8262				 * blocks, though: these are treated as favorable blocks.  The
8263				 * decay process is only applied when the next block in line
8264				 * is not a favorable/contiguous block.  This is not an
8265				 * exception to the general rule; we still insist on finding
8266				 * at least one deletable item per block accessed.  See
8267				 * bottomup_nblocksfavorable() for full details of the theory
8268				 * behind favorable blocks and heap block locality in general.
8269				 *
8270				 * Note: The first block in line is always treated as a
8271				 * favorable block, so the earliest possible point that the
8272				 * decay can be applied is just before we access the second
8273				 * block in line.  The Assert() verifies this for us.
8274				 */
8275				Assert(nblocksaccessed > 0 || nblocksfavorable > 0);
8276				if (nblocksfavorable > 0)
8277					nblocksfavorable--;
8278				else
8279					curtargetfreespace /= 2;
8280			}
8281
8282			/* release old buffer */
8283			if (BufferIsValid(buf))
8284				UnlockReleaseBuffer(buf);
8285
8286			blkno = ItemPointerGetBlockNumber(htid);
8287			buf = ReadBuffer(rel, blkno);
8288			nblocksaccessed++;
8289			Assert(!delstate->bottomup ||
8290				   nblocksaccessed <= BOTTOMUP_MAX_NBLOCKS);
8291
8292#ifdef USE_PREFETCH
8293
8294			/*
8295			 * To maintain the prefetch distance, prefetch one more page for
8296			 * each page we read.
8297			 */
8298			index_delete_prefetch_buffer(rel, &prefetch_state, 1);
8299#endif
8300
8301			LockBuffer(buf, BUFFER_LOCK_SHARE);
8302
8303			page = BufferGetPage(buf);
8304			maxoff = PageGetMaxOffsetNumber(page);
8305		}
8306
8307		/*
8308		 * In passing, detect index corruption involving an index page with a
8309		 * TID that points to a location in the heap that couldn't possibly be
8310		 * correct.  We only do this with actual TIDs from caller's index page
8311		 * (not items reached by traversing through a HOT chain).
8312		 */
8313		index_delete_check_htid(delstate, page, maxoff, htid, istatus);
8314
8315		if (istatus->knowndeletable)
8316			Assert(!delstate->bottomup && !istatus->promising);
8317		else
8318		{
8319			ItemPointerData tmp = *htid;
8320			HeapTupleData heapTuple;
8321
8322			/* Are any tuples from this HOT chain non-vacuumable? */
8323			if (heap_hot_search_buffer(&tmp, rel, buf, &SnapshotNonVacuumable,
8324									   &heapTuple, NULL, true))
8325				continue;		/* can't delete entry */
8326
8327			/* Caller will delete, since whole HOT chain is vacuumable */
8328			istatus->knowndeletable = true;
8329
8330			/* Maintain index free space info for bottom-up deletion case */
8331			if (delstate->bottomup)
8332			{
8333				Assert(istatus->freespace > 0);
8334				actualfreespace += istatus->freespace;
8335				if (actualfreespace >= curtargetfreespace)
8336					bottomup_final_block = true;
8337			}
8338		}
8339
8340		/*
8341		 * Maintain snapshotConflictHorizon value for deletion operation as a
8342		 * whole by advancing current value using heap tuple headers.  This is
8343		 * loosely based on the logic for pruning a HOT chain.
8344		 */
8345		offnum = ItemPointerGetOffsetNumber(htid);
8346		priorXmax = InvalidTransactionId;	/* cannot check first XMIN */
8347		for (;;)
8348		{
8349			ItemId		lp;
8350			HeapTupleHeader htup;
8351
8352			/* Sanity check (pure paranoia) */
8353			if (offnum < FirstOffsetNumber)
8354				break;
8355
8356			/*
8357			 * An offset past the end of page's line pointer array is possible
8358			 * when the array was truncated
8359			 */
8360			if (offnum > maxoff)
8361				break;
8362
8363			lp = PageGetItemId(page, offnum);
8364			if (ItemIdIsRedirected(lp))
8365			{
8366				offnum = ItemIdGetRedirect(lp);
8367				continue;
8368			}
8369
8370			/*
8371			 * We'll often encounter LP_DEAD line pointers (especially with an
8372			 * entry marked knowndeletable by our caller up front).  No heap
8373			 * tuple headers get examined for an htid that leads us to an
8374			 * LP_DEAD item.  This is okay because the earlier pruning
8375			 * operation that made the line pointer LP_DEAD in the first place
8376			 * must have considered the original tuple header as part of
8377			 * generating its own snapshotConflictHorizon value.
8378			 *
8379			 * Relying on XLOG_HEAP2_PRUNE_VACUUM_SCAN records like this is
8380			 * the same strategy that index vacuuming uses in all cases. Index
8381			 * VACUUM WAL records don't even have a snapshotConflictHorizon
8382			 * field of their own for this reason.
8383			 */
8384			if (!ItemIdIsNormal(lp))
8385				break;
8386
8387			htup = (HeapTupleHeader) PageGetItem(page, lp);
8388
8389			/*
8390			 * Check the tuple XMIN against prior XMAX, if any
8391			 */
8392			if (TransactionIdIsValid(priorXmax) &&
8393				!TransactionIdEquals(HeapTupleHeaderGetXmin(htup), priorXmax))
8394				break;
8395
8396			HeapTupleHeaderAdvanceConflictHorizon(htup,
8397												  &snapshotConflictHorizon);
8398
8399			/*
8400			 * If the tuple is not HOT-updated, then we are at the end of this
8401			 * HOT-chain.  No need to visit later tuples from the same update
8402			 * chain (they get their own index entries) -- just move on to
8403			 * next htid from index AM caller.
8404			 */
8405			if (!HeapTupleHeaderIsHotUpdated(htup))
8406				break;
8407
8408			/* Advance to next HOT chain member */
8409			Assert(ItemPointerGetBlockNumber(&htup->t_ctid) == blkno);
8410			offnum = ItemPointerGetOffsetNumber(&htup->t_ctid);
8411			priorXmax = HeapTupleHeaderGetUpdateXid(htup);
8412		}
8413
8414		/* Enable further/final shrinking of deltids for caller */
8415		finalndeltids = i + 1;
8416	}
8417
8418	UnlockReleaseBuffer(buf);
8419
8420	/*
8421	 * Shrink deltids array to exclude non-deletable entries at the end.  This
8422	 * is not just a minor optimization.  Final deltids array size might be
8423	 * zero for a bottom-up caller.  Index AM is explicitly allowed to rely on
8424	 * ndeltids being zero in all cases with zero total deletable entries.
8425	 */
8426	Assert(finalndeltids > 0 || delstate->bottomup);
8427	delstate->ndeltids = finalndeltids;
8428
8429	return snapshotConflictHorizon;
8430}
8431
8432/*
8433 * Specialized inlineable comparison function for index_delete_sort()
8434 */
8435static inline int
8436index_delete_sort_cmp(TM_IndexDelete *deltid1, TM_IndexDelete *deltid2)
8437{
8438	ItemPointer tid1 = &deltid1->tid;
8439	ItemPointer tid2 = &deltid2->tid;
8440
8441	{
8442		BlockNumber blk1 = ItemPointerGetBlockNumber(tid1);
8443		BlockNumber blk2 = ItemPointerGetBlockNumber(tid2);
8444
8445		if (blk1 != blk2)
8446			return (blk1 < blk2) ? -1 : 1;
8447	}
8448	{
8449		OffsetNumber pos1 = ItemPointerGetOffsetNumber(tid1);
8450		OffsetNumber pos2 = ItemPointerGetOffsetNumber(tid2);
8451
8452		if (pos1 != pos2)
8453			return (pos1 < pos2) ? -1 : 1;
8454	}
8455
8456	Assert(false);
8457
8458	return 0;
8459}
8460
8461/*
8462 * Sort deltids array from delstate by TID.  This prepares it for further
8463 * processing by heap_index_delete_tuples().
8464 *
8465 * This operation becomes a noticeable consumer of CPU cycles with some
8466 * workloads, so we go to the trouble of specialization/micro optimization.
8467 * We use shellsort for this because it's easy to specialize, compiles to
8468 * relatively few instructions, and is adaptive to presorted inputs/subsets
8469 * (which are typical here).
8470 */
8471static void
8472index_delete_sort(TM_IndexDeleteOp *delstate)
8473{
8474	TM_IndexDelete *deltids = delstate->deltids;
8475	int			ndeltids = delstate->ndeltids;
8476
8477	/*
8478	 * Shellsort gap sequence (taken from Sedgewick-Incerpi paper).
8479	 *
8480	 * This implementation is fast with array sizes up to ~4500.  This covers
8481	 * all supported BLCKSZ values.
8482	 */
8483	const int	gaps[9] = {1968, 861, 336, 112, 48, 21, 7, 3, 1};
8484
8485	/* Think carefully before changing anything here -- keep swaps cheap */
8486	StaticAssertDecl(sizeof(TM_IndexDelete) <= 8,
8487					 "element size exceeds 8 bytes");
8488
8489	for (int g = 0; g < lengthof(gaps); g++)
8490	{
8491		for (int hi = gaps[g], i = hi; i < ndeltids; i++)
8492		{
8493			TM_IndexDelete d = deltids[i];
8494			int			j = i;
8495
8496			while (j >= hi && index_delete_sort_cmp(&deltids[j - hi], &d) >= 0)
8497			{
8498				deltids[j] = deltids[j - hi];
8499				j -= hi;
8500			}
8501			deltids[j] = d;
8502		}
8503	}
8504}
8505
8506/*
8507 * Returns how many blocks should be considered favorable/contiguous for a
8508 * bottom-up index deletion pass.  This is a number of heap blocks that starts
8509 * from and includes the first block in line.
8510 *
8511 * There is always at least one favorable block during bottom-up index
8512 * deletion.  In the worst case (i.e. with totally random heap blocks) the
8513 * first block in line (the only favorable block) can be thought of as a
8514 * degenerate array of contiguous blocks that consists of a single block.
8515 * heap_index_delete_tuples() will expect this.
8516 *
8517 * Caller passes blockgroups, a description of the final order that deltids
8518 * will be sorted in for heap_index_delete_tuples() bottom-up index deletion
8519 * processing.  Note that deltids need not actually be sorted just yet (caller
8520 * only passes deltids to us so that we can interpret blockgroups).
8521 *
8522 * You might guess that the existence of contiguous blocks cannot matter much,
8523 * since in general the main factor that determines which blocks we visit is
8524 * the number of promising TIDs, which is a fixed hint from the index AM.
8525 * We're not really targeting the general case, though -- the actual goal is
8526 * to adapt our behavior to a wide variety of naturally occurring conditions.
8527 * The effects of most of the heuristics we apply are only noticeable in the
8528 * aggregate, over time and across many _related_ bottom-up index deletion
8529 * passes.
8530 *
8531 * Deeming certain blocks favorable allows heapam to recognize and adapt to
8532 * workloads where heap blocks visited during bottom-up index deletion can be
8533 * accessed contiguously, in the sense that each newly visited block is the
8534 * neighbor of the block that bottom-up deletion just finished processing (or
8535 * close enough to it).  It will likely be cheaper to access more favorable
8536 * blocks sooner rather than later (e.g. in this pass, not across a series of
8537 * related bottom-up passes).  Either way it is probably only a matter of time
8538 * (or a matter of further correlated version churn) before all blocks that
8539 * appear together as a single large batch of favorable blocks get accessed by
8540 * _some_ bottom-up pass.  Large batches of favorable blocks tend to either
8541 * appear almost constantly or not even once (it all depends on per-index
8542 * workload characteristics).
8543 *
8544 * Note that the blockgroups sort order applies a power-of-two bucketing
8545 * scheme that creates opportunities for contiguous groups of blocks to get
8546 * batched together, at least with workloads that are naturally amenable to
8547 * being driven by heap block locality.  This doesn't just enhance the spatial
8548 * locality of bottom-up heap block processing in the obvious way.  It also
8549 * enables temporal locality of access, since sorting by heap block number
8550 * naturally tends to make the bottom-up processing order deterministic.
8551 *
8552 * Consider the following example to get a sense of how temporal locality
8553 * might matter: There is a heap relation with several indexes, each of which
8554 * is low to medium cardinality.  It is subject to constant non-HOT updates.
8555 * The updates are skewed (in one part of the primary key, perhaps).  None of
8556 * the indexes are logically modified by the UPDATE statements (if they were
8557 * then bottom-up index deletion would not be triggered in the first place).
8558 * Naturally, each new round of index tuples (for each heap tuple that gets a
8559 * heap_update() call) will have the same heap TID in each and every index.
8560 * Since these indexes are low cardinality and never get logically modified,
8561 * heapam processing during bottom-up deletion passes will access heap blocks
8562 * in approximately sequential order.  Temporal locality of access occurs due
8563 * to bottom-up deletion passes behaving very similarly across each of the
8564 * indexes at any given moment.  This keeps the number of buffer misses needed
8565 * to visit heap blocks to a minimum.
8566 */
8567static int
8568bottomup_nblocksfavorable(IndexDeleteCounts *blockgroups, int nblockgroups,
8569						  TM_IndexDelete *deltids)
8570{
8571	int64		lastblock = -1;
8572	int			nblocksfavorable = 0;
8573
8574	Assert(nblockgroups >= 1);
8575	Assert(nblockgroups <= BOTTOMUP_MAX_NBLOCKS);
8576
8577	/*
8578	 * We tolerate heap blocks that will be accessed only slightly out of
8579	 * physical order.  Small blips occur when a pair of almost-contiguous
8580	 * blocks happen to fall into different buckets (perhaps due only to a
8581	 * small difference in npromisingtids that the bucketing scheme didn't
8582	 * quite manage to ignore).  We effectively ignore these blips by applying
8583	 * a small tolerance.  The precise tolerance we use is a little arbitrary,
8584	 * but it works well enough in practice.
8585	 */
8586	for (int b = 0; b < nblockgroups; b++)
8587	{
8588		IndexDeleteCounts *group = blockgroups + b;
8589		TM_IndexDelete *firstdtid = deltids + group->ifirsttid;
8590		BlockNumber block = ItemPointerGetBlockNumber(&firstdtid->tid);
8591
8592		if (lastblock != -1 &&
8593			((int64) block < lastblock - BOTTOMUP_TOLERANCE_NBLOCKS ||
8594			 (int64) block > lastblock + BOTTOMUP_TOLERANCE_NBLOCKS))
8595			break;
8596
8597		nblocksfavorable++;
8598		lastblock = block;
8599	}
8600
8601	/* Always indicate that there is at least 1 favorable block */
8602	Assert(nblocksfavorable >= 1);
8603
8604	return nblocksfavorable;
8605}
8606
8607/*
8608 * qsort comparison function for bottomup_sort_and_shrink()
8609 */
8610static int
8611bottomup_sort_and_shrink_cmp(const void *arg1, const void *arg2)
8612{
8613	const IndexDeleteCounts *group1 = (const IndexDeleteCounts *) arg1;
8614	const IndexDeleteCounts *group2 = (const IndexDeleteCounts *) arg2;
8615
8616	/*
8617	 * Most significant field is npromisingtids (which we invert the order of
8618	 * so as to sort in desc order).
8619	 *
8620	 * Caller should have already normalized npromisingtids fields into
8621	 * power-of-two values (buckets).
8622	 */
8623	if (group1->npromisingtids > group2->npromisingtids)
8624		return -1;
8625	if (group1->npromisingtids < group2->npromisingtids)
8626		return 1;
8627
8628	/*
8629	 * Tiebreak: desc ntids sort order.
8630	 *
8631	 * We cannot expect power-of-two values for ntids fields.  We should
8632	 * behave as if they were already rounded up for us instead.
8633	 */
8634	if (group1->ntids != group2->ntids)
8635	{
8636		uint32		ntids1 = pg_nextpower2_32((uint32) group1->ntids);
8637		uint32		ntids2 = pg_nextpower2_32((uint32) group2->ntids);
8638
8639		if (ntids1 > ntids2)
8640			return -1;
8641		if (ntids1 < ntids2)
8642			return 1;
8643	}
8644
8645	/*
8646	 * Tiebreak: asc offset-into-deltids-for-block (offset to first TID for
8647	 * block in deltids array) order.
8648	 *
8649	 * This is equivalent to sorting in ascending heap block number order
8650	 * (among otherwise equal subsets of the array).  This approach allows us
8651	 * to avoid accessing the out-of-line TID.  (We rely on the assumption
8652	 * that the deltids array was sorted in ascending heap TID order when
8653	 * these offsets to the first TID from each heap block group were formed.)
8654	 */
8655	if (group1->ifirsttid > group2->ifirsttid)
8656		return 1;
8657	if (group1->ifirsttid < group2->ifirsttid)
8658		return -1;
8659
8660	pg_unreachable();
8661
8662	return 0;
8663}
8664
8665/*
8666 * heap_index_delete_tuples() helper function for bottom-up deletion callers.
8667 *
8668 * Sorts deltids array in the order needed for useful processing by bottom-up
8669 * deletion.  The array should already be sorted in TID order when we're
8670 * called.  The sort process groups heap TIDs from deltids into heap block
8671 * groupings.  Earlier/more-promising groups/blocks are usually those that are
8672 * known to have the most "promising" TIDs.
8673 *
8674 * Sets new size of deltids array (ndeltids) in state.  deltids will only have
8675 * TIDs from the BOTTOMUP_MAX_NBLOCKS most promising heap blocks when we
8676 * return.  This often means that deltids will be shrunk to a small fraction
8677 * of its original size (we eliminate many heap blocks from consideration for
8678 * caller up front).
8679 *
8680 * Returns the number of "favorable" blocks.  See bottomup_nblocksfavorable()
8681 * for a definition and full details.
8682 */
8683static int
8684bottomup_sort_and_shrink(TM_IndexDeleteOp *delstate)
8685{
8686	IndexDeleteCounts *blockgroups;
8687	TM_IndexDelete *reordereddeltids;
8688	BlockNumber curblock = InvalidBlockNumber;
8689	int			nblockgroups = 0;
8690	int			ncopied = 0;
8691	int			nblocksfavorable = 0;
8692
8693	Assert(delstate->bottomup);
8694	Assert(delstate->ndeltids > 0);
8695
8696	/* Calculate per-heap-block count of TIDs */
8697	blockgroups = palloc(sizeof(IndexDeleteCounts) * delstate->ndeltids);
8698	for (int i = 0; i < delstate->ndeltids; i++)
8699	{
8700		TM_IndexDelete *ideltid = &delstate->deltids[i];
8701		TM_IndexStatus *istatus = delstate->status + ideltid->id;
8702		ItemPointer htid = &ideltid->tid;
8703		bool		promising = istatus->promising;
8704
8705		if (curblock != ItemPointerGetBlockNumber(htid))
8706		{
8707			/* New block group */
8708			nblockgroups++;
8709
8710			Assert(curblock < ItemPointerGetBlockNumber(htid) ||
8711				   !BlockNumberIsValid(curblock));
8712
8713			curblock = ItemPointerGetBlockNumber(htid);
8714			blockgroups[nblockgroups - 1].ifirsttid = i;
8715			blockgroups[nblockgroups - 1].ntids = 1;
8716			blockgroups[nblockgroups - 1].npromisingtids = 0;
8717		}
8718		else
8719		{
8720			blockgroups[nblockgroups - 1].ntids++;
8721		}
8722
8723		if (promising)
8724			blockgroups[nblockgroups - 1].npromisingtids++;
8725	}
8726
8727	/*
8728	 * We're about ready to sort block groups to determine the optimal order
8729	 * for visiting heap blocks.  But before we do, round the number of
8730	 * promising tuples for each block group up to the next power-of-two,
8731	 * unless it is very low (less than 4), in which case we round up to 4.
8732	 * npromisingtids is far too noisy to trust when choosing between a pair
8733	 * of block groups that both have very low values.
8734	 *
8735	 * This scheme divides heap blocks/block groups into buckets.  Each bucket
8736	 * contains blocks that have _approximately_ the same number of promising
8737	 * TIDs as each other.  The goal is to ignore relatively small differences
8738	 * in the total number of promising entries, so that the whole process can
8739	 * give a little weight to heapam factors (like heap block locality)
8740	 * instead.  This isn't a trade-off, really -- we have nothing to lose. It
8741	 * would be foolish to interpret small differences in npromisingtids
8742	 * values as anything more than noise.
8743	 *
8744	 * We tiebreak on nhtids when sorting block group subsets that have the
8745	 * same npromisingtids, but this has the same issues as npromisingtids,
8746	 * and so nhtids is subject to the same power-of-two bucketing scheme. The
8747	 * only reason that we don't fix nhtids in the same way here too is that
8748	 * we'll need accurate nhtids values after the sort.  We handle nhtids
8749	 * bucketization dynamically instead (in the sort comparator).
8750	 *
8751	 * See bottomup_nblocksfavorable() for a full explanation of when and how
8752	 * heap locality/favorable blocks can significantly influence when and how
8753	 * heap blocks are accessed.
8754	 */
8755	for (int b = 0; b < nblockgroups; b++)
8756	{
8757		IndexDeleteCounts *group = blockgroups + b;
8758
8759		/* Better off falling back on nhtids with low npromisingtids */
8760		if (group->npromisingtids <= 4)
8761			group->npromisingtids = 4;
8762		else
8763			group->npromisingtids =
8764				pg_nextpower2_32((uint32) group->npromisingtids);
8765	}
8766
8767	/* Sort groups and rearrange caller's deltids array */
8768	qsort(blockgroups, nblockgroups, sizeof(IndexDeleteCounts),
8769		  bottomup_sort_and_shrink_cmp);
8770	reordereddeltids = palloc(delstate->ndeltids * sizeof(TM_IndexDelete));
8771
8772	nblockgroups = Min(BOTTOMUP_MAX_NBLOCKS, nblockgroups);
8773	/* Determine number of favorable blocks at the start of final deltids */
8774	nblocksfavorable = bottomup_nblocksfavorable(blockgroups, nblockgroups,
8775												 delstate->deltids);
8776
8777	for (int b = 0; b < nblockgroups; b++)
8778	{
8779		IndexDeleteCounts *group = blockgroups + b;
8780		TM_IndexDelete *firstdtid = delstate->deltids + group->ifirsttid;
8781
8782		memcpy(reordereddeltids + ncopied, firstdtid,
8783			   sizeof(TM_IndexDelete) * group->ntids);
8784		ncopied += group->ntids;
8785	}
8786
8787	/* Copy final grouped and sorted TIDs back into start of caller's array */
8788	memcpy(delstate->deltids, reordereddeltids,
8789		   sizeof(TM_IndexDelete) * ncopied);
8790	delstate->ndeltids = ncopied;
8791
8792	pfree(reordereddeltids);
8793	pfree(blockgroups);
8794
8795	return nblocksfavorable;
8796}
8797
8798/*
8799 * Perform XLogInsert for a heap-visible operation.  'block' is the block
8800 * being marked all-visible, and vm_buffer is the buffer containing the
8801 * corresponding visibility map block.  Both should have already been modified
8802 * and dirtied.
8803 *
8804 * snapshotConflictHorizon comes from the largest xmin on the page being
8805 * marked all-visible.  REDO routine uses it to generate recovery conflicts.
8806 *
8807 * If checksums or wal_log_hints are enabled, we may also generate a full-page
8808 * image of heap_buffer. Otherwise, we optimize away the FPI (by specifying
8809 * REGBUF_NO_IMAGE for the heap buffer), in which case the caller should *not*
8810 * update the heap page's LSN.
8811 */
8812XLogRecPtr
8813log_heap_visible(Relation rel, Buffer heap_buffer, Buffer vm_buffer,
8814				 TransactionId snapshotConflictHorizon, uint8 vmflags)
8815{
8816	xl_heap_visible xlrec;
8817	XLogRecPtr	recptr;
8818	uint8		flags;
8819
8820	Assert(BufferIsValid(heap_buffer));
8821	Assert(BufferIsValid(vm_buffer));
8822
8823	xlrec.snapshotConflictHorizon = snapshotConflictHorizon;
8824	xlrec.flags = vmflags;
8825	if (RelationIsAccessibleInLogicalDecoding(rel))
8826		xlrec.flags |= VISIBILITYMAP_XLOG_CATALOG_REL;
8827	XLogBeginInsert();
8828	XLogRegisterData(&xlrec, SizeOfHeapVisible);
8829
8830	XLogRegisterBuffer(0, vm_buffer, 0);
8831
8832	flags = REGBUF_STANDARD;
8833	if (!XLogHintBitIsNeeded())
8834		flags |= REGBUF_NO_IMAGE;
8835	XLogRegisterBuffer(1, heap_buffer, flags);
8836
8837	recptr = XLogInsert(RM_HEAP2_ID, XLOG_HEAP2_VISIBLE);
8838
8839	return recptr;
8840}
8841
8842/*
8843 * Perform XLogInsert for a heap-update operation.  Caller must already
8844 * have modified the buffer(s) and marked them dirty.
8845 */
8846static XLogRecPtr
8847log_heap_update(Relation reln, Buffer oldbuf,
8848				Buffer newbuf, HeapTuple oldtup, HeapTuple newtup,
8849				HeapTuple old_key_tuple,
8850				bool all_visible_cleared, bool new_all_visible_cleared)
8851{
8852	xl_heap_update xlrec;
8853	xl_heap_header xlhdr;
8854	xl_heap_header xlhdr_idx;
8855	uint8		info;
8856	uint16		prefix_suffix[2];
8857	uint16		prefixlen = 0,
8858				suffixlen = 0;
8859	XLogRecPtr	recptr;
8860	Page		page = BufferGetPage(newbuf);
8861	bool		need_tuple_data = RelationIsLogicallyLogged(reln);
8862	bool		init;
8863	int			bufflags;
8864
8865	/* Caller should not call me on a non-WAL-logged relation */
8866	Assert(RelationNeedsWAL(reln));
8867
8868	XLogBeginInsert();
8869
8870	if (HeapTupleIsHeapOnly(newtup))
8871		info = XLOG_HEAP_HOT_UPDATE;
8872	else
8873		info = XLOG_HEAP_UPDATE;
8874
8875	/*
8876	 * If the old and new tuple are on the same page, we only need to log the
8877	 * parts of the new tuple that were changed.  That saves on the amount of
8878	 * WAL we need to write.  Currently, we just count any unchanged bytes in
8879	 * the beginning and end of the tuple.  That's quick to check, and
8880	 * perfectly covers the common case that only one field is updated.
8881	 *
8882	 * We could do this even if the old and new tuple are on different pages,
8883	 * but only if we don't make a full-page image of the old page, which is
8884	 * difficult to know in advance.  Also, if the old tuple is corrupt for
8885	 * some reason, it would allow the corruption to propagate the new page,
8886	 * so it seems best to avoid.  Under the general assumption that most
8887	 * updates tend to create the new tuple version on the same page, there
8888	 * isn't much to be gained by doing this across pages anyway.
8889	 *
8890	 * Skip this if we're taking a full-page image of the new page, as we
8891	 * don't include the new tuple in the WAL record in that case.  Also
8892	 * disable if wal_level='logical', as logical decoding needs to be able to
8893	 * read the new tuple in whole from the WAL record alone.
8894	 */
8895	if (oldbuf == newbuf && !need_tuple_data &&
8896		!XLogCheckBufferNeedsBackup(newbuf))
8897	{
8898		char	   *oldp = (char *) oldtup->t_data + oldtup->t_data->t_hoff;
8899		char	   *newp = (char *) newtup->t_data + newtup->t_data->t_hoff;
8900		int			oldlen = oldtup->t_len - oldtup->t_data->t_hoff;
8901		int			newlen = newtup->t_len - newtup->t_data->t_hoff;
8902
8903		/* Check for common prefix between old and new tuple */
8904		for (prefixlen = 0; prefixlen < Min(oldlen, newlen); prefixlen++)
8905		{
8906			if (newp[prefixlen] != oldp[prefixlen])
8907				break;
8908		}
8909
8910		/*
8911		 * Storing the length of the prefix takes 2 bytes, so we need to save
8912		 * at least 3 bytes or there's no point.
8913		 */
8914		if (prefixlen < 3)
8915			prefixlen = 0;
8916
8917		/* Same for suffix */
8918		for (suffixlen = 0; suffixlen < Min(oldlen, newlen) - prefixlen; suffixlen++)
8919		{
8920			if (newp[newlen - suffixlen - 1] != oldp[oldlen - suffixlen - 1])
8921				break;
8922		}
8923		if (suffixlen < 3)
8924			suffixlen = 0;
8925	}
8926
8927	/* Prepare main WAL data chain */
8928	xlrec.flags = 0;
8929	if (all_visible_cleared)
8930		xlrec.flags |= XLH_UPDATE_OLD_ALL_VISIBLE_CLEARED;
8931	if (new_all_visible_cleared)
8932		xlrec.flags |= XLH_UPDATE_NEW_ALL_VISIBLE_CLEARED;
8933	if (prefixlen > 0)
8934		xlrec.flags |= XLH_UPDATE_PREFIX_FROM_OLD;
8935	if (suffixlen > 0)
